title: 埃及十字架之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\27-埃及十字架之谜-埃勒里奎因.txt
summary:
On Christmas morning in the village of Aroo, West Virginia, the decapitated body of Andrew Van, an eccentric school principal, is found nailed to a T-shaped signpost at a crossroads. The crime is marked by a recurring "T" motif, including a bloodstain on Van's door. Van's servant, Clinton, has also disappeared, and a long-handled axe belonging to Clinton is found at the scene without fingerprints. Ellery Queen and his father, Inspector Quin, investigate the scene, noting that Van's head is missing.

The investigation reveals that Van was an American citizen previously living in Pittsburgh, where records identified him as Armenian. During legal proceedings, it is noted that Van lived a reclusive life, kept his salary in cash, and requested unusual philosophical books. A witness, Casper Crocker, describes a dark-skinned, limping man carrying a shovel near the area on Christmas Eve. Another witness, a traveling medicine showman named Harakti (who claims to be the god Horus), provides erratic testimony. Ellery recognizes a Uraeus emblem on Harakti's hand, linking him to ancient Egypt. Harakti identifies his "disciple," a man named Verga Krossak, as his business manager, but Krossak has since disappeared. Ellery suggests the "T" symbol is the Tau cross, also known as the "Egyptian cross." The case eventually fades from the news, and Ellery returns to New York.

Six months later, Ellery is summoned by his former professor, Yadley, to investigate a second murder in Bradwood, Long Island. The victim, millionaire Thomas Braid, is found decapitated and bound to a nine-foot-tall totem pole shaped like a capital "T." A red wooden chess piece is found near the body, and Ellery discovers a matching red mark on Braid's palm, suggesting the piece was pressed into his skin. In a nearby gazebo, Ellery finds a large "T" painted in blood and a stone pipe containing cubed tobacco. Ellery hypothesizes that Braid was attacked while carrying the chess piece to the gazebo.

The investigation into Braid's death involves his widow, Margaret, his daughter Helen, and his business associate, Jonah Lincoln. Lincoln reveals that Braid had a past conflict with Paul Roman, a man associated with a "Sun Cult" on Oyster Island led by Harakat. Ellery notes a pattern of pseudonyms used by the victims—Thomas Braid, Stephen Megalos, and Andrew Van—all corresponding to geographical locations. He also discovers that Braid was an avid chess player. During a search of Braid's study, Ellery and Professor Yadley find a hidden note inside a piano. The note, written by Braid, expresses fear for his life and claims that Andrew Van is actually alive and that only Stephen Megalos knows his location.

The investigation shifts to Oyster Island, where the "Sun Cult" resides. The group encounters Harakat and Paul Roman. Professor Yadley reveals that Harakat is actually a former Egyptologist named Striker who suffered mental derangement. The island is also home to a nudist colony. A confrontation occurs between Roman and Jonah Lincoln, leading to Roman's arrest and a subsequent investigation into Mrs. Braid, who admits to having an affair with Roman.

The tension escalates when the Reins, a British couple living nearby, are revealed to be international jewel thieves. They assault Dr. Temple and flee. Meanwhile, a trap is set to lure the suspected killer, Croscak, out of hiding. The plan goes catastrophically wrong: Captain Swift is found unconscious, and Stephen Megalos is found dead, his headless body tied to the mast of his yacht, the *Helen*, in a manner resembling a crucifixion. A "T" is written in blood on the cabin door.

Ellery realizes the killer is likely a man with a limp. A search of the area reveals a series of footprints that suggest the killer's right heel sinks deeper, indicating a heavy limp. Ellery also finds a used bandage and an unlabeled bottle of iodine at a remote mountain shack. He deduces that the killer injured his own wrist and used the unlabeled bottle because he was familiar with its contents.

A multi-state manhunt follows, leading Ellery to Chicago, where he and a group of investigators corner a man in a hotel. However, the man is not the killer, but the "dead" Andrew Van, who has been hiding in plain sight.

In the final resolution, Ellery reveals the truth: there was no "fanatic" killer. The entire series of murders was orchestrated by Andrew Van (also known as Andrew Tewell) to claim an inheritance and settle a generational blood feud with the Croscak family. Van had murdered the real Croscak in Aroo, using the "T" motif and decapitations to make the deaths appear to be the work of a religious maniac. He then used the missing head of the first victim to hide his own identity. Van killed Clinton to cover his tracks and later killed Thomas Braid and Stephen Megalos to maintain the illusion of a vengeful ghost. He even staged his own "death" and "return" to manipulate the inheritance and protect himself. The "limp" was a deception, and the various religious symbols were mere distractions. Ellery concludes that the "Egyptian Cross" was a tool of calculated deception rather than religious madness.

title: 有翼之暗
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\79-有翼之暗-麻耶雄嵩.txt
summary:
In the wintry landscape of the northern Kyoto Basin, investigators Muto and Mutsuji arrive at Inkyou Castle, a Gothic-style mansion known as Sparrow Castle. The atmosphere is heavy with police presence, signaling a murder investigation involving the wealthy Ito family. The mansion, characterized by a pervasive red color scheme and a museum-like collection of ancient art, serves as the stage for a series of gruesome, theatrical crimes.

The investigation begins with the discovery of the decapitated and mutilated body of Mr. Mikage Ito. The crime is bizarre: his feet were severed to fit him into decorative medieval iron armor boots. While searching for his missing head, investigators find a head on a coat rack, but it belongs to a man named Hayashi, not Ito. This discovery suggests the killer is intentionally setting traps. The investigation moves to a windowless stone room called "Hell's Gate," which contains a guillotine and presents a "locked-room" mystery. Inside, the headless corpse of a man named Uma is found, holding a complex brass key in his left hand despite being right-handed.

As the body count rises, the murders follow a distinct pattern. The killer decapitates victims and leaves body parts in conspicuous locations, seemingly performing for an audience. The narrator, a mystery novelist, and the brilliant detective Mochizuki observe that the crimes appear to be imitations of the "nationality series" by Ellery Queen, such as *The Chinese Orange Mystery* and *The Roman Hat Mystery*. The victims include family members, servants, and even the detective McArthur, whose head is found on a table.

Mochizuki and Muto propose various theories, including the idea that the killer is using "situation construction" to manipulate the investigation. They suggest the killer is a genius treating the mansion like a crossword puzzle. A complex scientific hypothesis is even proposed to explain the first locked-room mystery: a freak accident during a simultaneous decapitation of Ito and another man created a temporary "Frankenstein" entity, where Ito's consciousness briefly inhabited the other man's body, allowing him to lock the door from the inside before dying.

The true nature of the crimes is revealed through two distinct layers of deception. First, Mochizuki identifies the primary perpetrator as Kiri (also known as Misty or Mistuho), a woman with a twisted Oedipus complex. Driven by a fanatical desire to transform her associate Kaji into a "god," she orchestrated the murders as a "holy war" to purify the bloodline and fulfill a religious delusion inspired by an occult book. She used the Ellery Queen themes to mask her movements and create a sense of divine inevitability. She intended to eliminate other family members to grant Kaji absolute power and legitimacy.

The second, even more profound layer of deception is uncovered by the narrator in the mansion's basement. He discovers that the elderly housekeeper, Nishimura, is actually the ninety-year-old Lady Hinata in disguise. Hinata had been manipulating the entire situation for decades, using her knowledge of the family's secrets to frame others and protect her own interests. She had even poisoned the former president, Oyama, and used the "locked-room" illusions to hide her own movements. She manipulated the investigation by planting false clues, such as a foreign LP record and citron pips, to lead investigators to false conclusions.

The final revelation comes from Kougetsu Joushou, who confronts the housekeeper, revealing her true identity as Anna-Stasia, a descendant of the Romanov bloodline. She had murdered the composer Medvedev to protect her secret identity and planned a massacre to prevent her lineage from being exposed. Kougetsu, revealed to be her biological grandson and the twin brother of the murdered McArthur, confronts her for her failures and her disgrace to their royal name. The narrative concludes with the collapse of the family's illusions, leaving behind a trail of broken legacies and the hollow remains of a manufactured divinity.

title: 阳光下的罪恶
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\117-阳光下的罪恶-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In 1782, Captain Roger Umpleby built a house on a headland off the coast of Loch Cardip. By 1922, his descendant Arthur Umpleby had transformed the property into the Captain's Flag Hotel, a resort featuring a concrete causeway, tennis courts, and a private bay. During the summer of 1922, Hercule Poirot is a guest at the hotel, seeking relaxation. Other guests include the Gardners, Miss Bustle, Colonel Barron, Major Barry, the vicar Stephen Lane, and the Redferns—Patrick and Christine.

The atmosphere at the resort is marked by discussions of social habits and the presence of evil. While some guests believe the tranquil location is safe, Poirot argues that evil exists everywhere, even under the sun. The arrival of Elena Marshall, a former stage actress with a magnetic and commanding presence, draws intense scrutiny. Many guests, including the vicar and Miss Bustle, view her as a menace or the embodiment of evil. Her husband, Kenneth Marshall, appears calm and indifferent to her arrival. Rosamund Darnley, a beautiful business owner and childhood friend of Kenneth, reveals to Poirot that Kenneth is a good but foolish man who repeatedly marries problematic women. She identifies Elena as a "gold digger" who has been involved in various scandals and is currently targeting Patrick Redfern, a man who appears devoted to his wife, Christine.

Tensions rise as Elena’s presence disrupts the lives of those around her. Kenneth’s teenage daughter, Linda Marshall, harbors a deep hatred for her stepmother, whom she views as a corrupting influence. Meanwhile, Christine Redfern confronts Patrick, accusing him of being infatuated with Elena. Elsewhere, Rosamund Darnley suggests to Kenneth that he should divorce Elena, but he insists on honoring his marriage commitment.

On the morning of August 25th, Elena tells Poirot she wishes to be alone on a raft. Later, while rowing near Elfin Bay, Patrick Redfern and Miss Bustle discover Elena’s body lying face down in the sand. She has been strangled. The investigation, led by Colonel Weston and various inspectors, considers several motives: Elena’s wealth, her history of attracting men, and potential blackmail. Suspects include Kenneth Marshall, who stands to inherit her estate; Patrick Redfern, who was seen with her; and various other guests. Kenneth provides an alibi involving a typewriter, which is later supported by Rosamund Darnley, though Poirot remains suspicious of her motives.

As the investigation progresses, several clues emerge: a sandwich box containing drugs found in a cave, a letter from a man named "J.N." expressing obsessive love, and remnants of a ritual in Linda Marshall's room. Poirot also notes a connection to unsolved strangulation cases involving husbands with airtight alibis, specifically the deaths of Alice Corey and Nellie Parsons.

The situation takes a dire turn when Linda Marshall is found unconscious after consuming sleeping pills, leaving a note confessing to the murder. However, Poirot realizes the truth is far more complex. He reveals that the murder was a meticulously planned operation carried out by Patrick and Christine Redfern. The pair are actually Edward and Christine Corey, serial killers who had been linked to previous unsolved strangulations.

Patrick and Christine used a sophisticated deception to commit the crime and frame others. Christine used brown sunscreen to alter her skin tone and wore a white swimsuit and a green hat to pose as a decoy corpse on the beach, tricking witnesses into seeing a "body" while the actual murder was taking place. They manipulated time by tampering with Linda’s watch and used the girl's superstitious nature to frame her, even burning evidence in her fireplace to make it appear she had performed a witchcraft ritual. Patrick killed Elena in a hidden cave, known as the "fairy hole," after she went there to meet him, believing he was her lover.

After Patrick attempts to strangle Poirot in a fit of rage, the truth is fully exposed. The killers' plan to use Linda as a scapegoat fails. In the aftermath, Kenneth Marshall confronts Rosamund Darnley, revealing he had suspected her of the crime due to her past. The novel concludes with the realization that the true evil was not the perceived wickedness of Elena Marshall, but the predatory nature of the Redferns.

title: 顺水推舟
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\118-顺水推舟-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In the early spring of 1946, Linnie "Lyn" Marchmont returns to her family's home, White House, in the village of Wormsley Valley following her service in the Women's Royal Naval Service. She finds her family's financial stability compromised by the death of her wealthy uncle, Gordon Claude. Because Gordon died without a will, his unexpected widow, Rosaline (also known as Mrs. Andeh), inherited his entire estate, leaving Lyn’s mother, Adela Marchmont, in significant debt.

The Claude family is in turmoil. Jeremy Claude, a lawyer, confesses to his wife, Francis, that he has embezzled a trust fund and needs money to avoid discovery. Francis, a pragmatic and loyal woman, suggests borrowing from the "enigmatic" Rosaline. Meanwhile, Rosaline’s brother, David Hunter, stays close to her, ostensibly to protect her from the resentful Claude family, though he appears to be manipulating her wealth.

Tensions escalate when a man using the alias Enoch Yates (later revealed to be Charles Trenton, a swindler and relative of Francis) arrives in the valley. He claims to have information that could ruin Rosaline: that her first husband, Robert André, is actually alive. If André is alive, Rosaline’s marriage to Gordon was bigamous, and her inheritance would be invalid. David Hunter, desperate to protect the secret and the money, enters into a blackmail negotiation with Yates.

The situation turns violent when Yates is found dead in Room 5 of the Stagg Inn, his head smashed with steel fire tongs. Rolly Claude, Lyn's fiancé, is questioned by the police; he admits to visiting the man but claims he left before the murder. The investigation, led by Superintendent Spence and later joined by Hercule Poirot, focuses on the possibility that David Hunter killed Yates to stop the blackmail. A witness, Colonel Porter, identifies the deceased as Robert André, but Porter later commits suicide, likely due to the pressure of his false testimony.

As the investigation deepens, Poirot observes several inconsistencies. He notes that the victim, Charles Trenton, was not actually Robert André, but a blackmailer. He also discovers that the "Rosaline" living at Frognack is not the real Rosaline, but Eileen Corrigan, an Irish maid whom David Hunter had convinced to impersonate his deceased sister to secure her wealth.

The truth is finally unraveled by Poirot. He reveals that the death of Charles Trenton was an accidental killing committed by Rolly Claude. Rolly had confronted the man in a rage after discovering his own uncle, Jeremy, had attempted to defraud Rosaline. In the struggle, Rolly punched the man, who died instantly. Rolly then tampered with the crime scene and the victim's watch to frame David Hunter and protect himself.

Furthermore, Poirot exposes David Hunter’s elaborate web of lies. To create an alibi for the night of the murder, Hunter had disguised himself as a woman in an orange scarf and heavy makeup to be seen by witnesses, making it appear as though he were elsewhere. When the imposter, Eileen Corrigan, began to suffer a mental breakdown due to her guilt, Hunter murdered her by replacing her bromide sleeping powder with morphine.

Following the exposure of these crimes, David Hunter is apprehended. Rolly Claude, consumed by guilt over the deaths of Trenton and Colonel Porter, is left to face the consequences of his actions. In the aftermath, Lyn chooses to stay with Rolly at his farm, dismissing his crimes as manslaughter and declaring that she has finally found her home.

title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot becomes involved in a series of unusual thefts at a student dormitory located at 26 Walnut Street on Chestnut Street. The investigation is prompted by the distress of his secretary, Miss Lemon, whose sister, Mrs. Haberdasher, serves as the dormitory supervisor. Mrs. Haberdasher reports a bizarre pattern of missing items, ranging from trivial objects like light bulbs and recipes to significant pieces like a stethoscope and a diamond engagement ring belonging to a student named Patricia Lean.

Poirot meets with Mrs. Haberdasher to review a list of stolen goods. He notes the strange randomness of the items, though he finds the theft of a stethoscope and a diamond ring particularly significant. The ring, which belonged to Patricia’s mother, had previously been discovered in a soup bowl belonging to another student, Valerie Hobhouse. As Poirot investigates, he learns about the residents, including the quiet Patricia Lean, the clever Valerie Hobhouse, the volatile Nigel Chapman, and the postgraduate student Colin McNaughton. Poirot also observes that some property damage, such as a torn canvas backpack and a cut silk scarf, suggests malice rather than simple theft.

Tensions rise within the dormitory when Elizabeth Johnston, a law student, discovers her thesis has been ruined by spilled green ink. Nigel Chapman is suspected because he uses green ink, but the atmosphere grows increasingly paranoid. Amidst this chaos, a student named Celia Austin confesses to the thefts, claiming she is a kleptomaniac. Colin McNaughton, a psychology student, adopts a protective role over her, arguing that her actions are a psychological manifestation of an emotional crisis. However, when Poirot presents a list of specific incidents, Celia denies involvement in several, including the theft of the stethoscope and the ink incident.

The mystery turns deadly when Celia Austin is found dead, apparently having committed suicide via a morphine overdose. While a suicide note is found, Mrs. Haberdasher realizes the note is actually a torn fragment of a previous letter, suggesting the death was a staged murder. Inspector Sharp takes over the investigation, noting that hyoscine morphine was stolen from the pharmacy where Celia worked. Suspects include various students and staff with access to the pharmacy, including Nigel Chapman and Leonard Bateson.

As the investigation deepens, more deaths occur. The landlady, Mrs. Nikolai, is found dead in the street; while initially thought to be a victim of alcoholism, she is suspected of being murdered. Shortly thereafter, Patricia Lean is found murdered in her room, having been struck with a marble paperweight. During the investigation, it is revealed that Nigel Chapman had been in possession of various poisons, which he had acquired through illegal methods, including stealing from a doctor's car, forging prescriptions, and posing as a medical intern.

Poirot begins to synthesize the disparate threads. He discovers that the "random" thefts and the destruction of property were part of a sophisticated smuggling operation involving drugs and precious gems. The smugglers used high-quality canvas backpacks with removable linings to transport contraband. Valerie Hobhouse is revealed to be a central figure in this operation, using Celia Austin’s kleptomania as a ruse to mask the movement of goods. Valerie also admits to stealing the diamond from Patricia Lean's ring to cover gambling debts.

Poirot identifies Nigel Chapman as the primary murderer. Nigel had changed his name to escape his past; he was actually Nigel Stanley, the son of the renowned chemist Arthur Stanley. Nigel had murdered his own mother years earlier by overdosing her on barbiturates to prevent her from revealing that he had forged her signature. He murdered Celia because she discovered his true identity and murdered Patricia because she pressured him to reconcile with his father. Nigel had attempted to frame others, such as planting Leonard Bateson's hair, but his overconfidence led to his downfall.

The investigation concludes with the discovery of a sealed letter from Arthur Stanley, which contained Nigel's confession to his mother's murder. Valerie Hobhouse, who is revealed to be Mrs. Nikolai's daughter, provides a statement to the police admitting she acted as an accessory to Nigel's smuggling crimes and the murders, seeking to bring him to justice.

title: 怪钟疑案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\124-怪钟疑案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
On September 9th, at 2:59 PM, Colin Lamb was walking on Wilbraham Crescent when a young girl, Hilary Webber, rushed out of house number 19 screaming that a man was dead inside. Lamb entered the house and met Millee Pema-Chhoe, a blind teacher, who had discovered a man's body behind her sofa. Detective Inspector Dick Hakast arrived to investigate the scene, where the man had been stabbed. The deceased was found with a business card for an "R. H. Kouri" of a non-existent insurance company. A strange discrepancy was noted: while the house's cuckoo clock struck three, several other clocks in the room—including a silver travel clock and a gilded French clock—were set nearly an hour ahead and all stopped at 4:13 PM.

The investigation initially focused on Hilary Webber, a stenographer from the Cavendish Document Printing Agency, who had been sent to the house by a request she claimed was made by Miss Pema-Chhoe. However, Miss Pema-Chhoe denied making any such call. As the police interviewed neighbors, including the Watrous family, the Lymes, the McNaughtons, and the eccentric Mrs. Hemm, a pattern of suspicious activity emerged. The gardens of the neighboring houses were interconnected, providing easy access to the crime scene. A ten-year-old girl named Geraldine, who observed the neighborhood through a telescope, provided a detailed timeline, noting that a large laundry basket from "Snowflake Laundry" had been delivered to the house while Miss Pema-Chhoe was out.

The mystery deepened with the death of Ina Brent, a colleague of Hilary Webber, who was found strangled in a phone booth on Wilbraham Crescent. Her death appeared linked to the investigation, as she had expressed doubts about the testimony provided during the inquest of the first victim. Meanwhile, a woman named Melina Rival identified the deceased man as her long-lost husband, Harry Castleton. However, Inspector Hakast discovered that the man's physical features, specifically a scar behind his ear, did not match the real Harry Castleton, suggesting the identification was a fraudulent attempt to protect someone's interests. It was later revealed that the man was actually Quentin Dugastlin.

Colin Lamb, seeking clarity, consulted the legendary detective Hercule Poirot. Poirot observed that the case's dramatic elements—the mysterious clocks, the fake insurance agent, and the blind witness—were distractions designed to obscure a simpler motive. He noted that the victim had been deliberately stripped of his identity. Poirot eventually deduced the truth: the murder was a sophisticated conspiracy orchestrated by Miss Martin (the head of the Cavendish Agency) and Valerie Brand. The two women were sisters.

The motive was a fraudulent inheritance scheme involving the Brand family. The Brands had used a previous marriage to claim a large inheritance from a Canadian relative, and they murdered Dugastlin to prevent him from exposing the truth. The complex arrangement of clocks and the use of Miss Pema-Chhoe's home as a headquarters were part of a plan to misdirect the police. The "Rosemary" clock, which Hilary Webber had taken from the scene, was actually her own property, and her involvement was a result of being lured into the situation.

In the aftermath, the criminal plot was dismantled. Valerie Brand confessed that her husband and sister had orchestrated the scheme. Miss Martin was identified as the person who had purchased the suspicious clocks. Following the resolution, Colin Lamb, having helped unravel the web of deception, decided to return to his career in marine biology in Australia.

title: 万圣节前夜的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\126-万圣节前夜的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Ariadne Oliver, a famous mystery novelist, attends a Halloween party in Woodleigh hosted by Royana Drecker. The event, intended for children graduating from Elm Tree School, includes various games such as an apple-biting contest and a "dragon hunt." During the festivities, thirteen-year-old Joyce Reynolds makes a startling claim to several adults, including Mrs. Oliver, that she once witnessed a murder. Shortly after the party concludes, Joyce is found dead in the library; she has been murdered by someone pushing her head into a heavy, zinc-lined bucket of water.

Distressed by the event, Mrs. Oliver seeks the help of Hercule Poirot. Poirot begins an investigation that connects the death of Joyce to several past incidents in the Woodleigh area. He learns that Joyce was a habitual liar, but he suspects her claim of witnessing a murder was true and that her death was a preemptive strike by a killer protecting a secret. Poirot consults with retired Superintendent Spence and his sister Elspeth, who provides local insights.

The investigation uncovers a web of historical crimes and forgeries. Poirot examines the death of Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith, a wealthy widow who died of apparent heart failure. Her estate was the subject of a legal dispute involving a codicil that favored a foreign exchange student, Olga Seminova. While lawyers claimed the codicil was a forgery, a cleaning lady named Harriet Lymon reveals she witnessed the widow sign a genuine document. This suggests that a forged version was introduced to disrupt the inheritance. Poirot also looks into the unsolved murder of a law clerk, Leslie Firrell, who was stabbed to death, and the strangulation of a teacher, Janet White.

Poirot's inquiries lead him to the Stone Mine Garden, a meticulously designed landscape created by Michael Garfield, who was financed by the late Mrs. Llewellyn-Smith. Poirot meets Garfield and observes his intense, almost obsessive devotion to aesthetic beauty. During his investigation, a second tragedy strikes: Leopold Reynolds, Joyce’s ten-year-old brother, is found drowned in a stream. Poirot deduces that Leopold was murdered to silence him, as he had likely witnessed the killer during Joyce's death.

The tension escalates when Miranda, the young daughter of Judith Butler and Ariadne Oliver's friend, goes missing. She is lured away by a man who takes her to the Canterbury Stone Circle under the guise of a ritual. Nicholas Lanchester intervenes to save her, revealing that the man is a murderer. Miranda subsequently confesses to Poirot that she had witnessed Mrs. Drecker and Michael Garfield carrying a body in the quarry garden years prior, an act Garfield had convinced her was a necessary ritual sacrifice.

In the final resolution, Poirot reveals that Royana Drecker and Michael Garfield were partners in a murderous scheme. Their motive was to secure the Llewellyn-Smith estate to fund Garfield's vision of a "new Eden" on a Greek island. They had murdered Olga Seminova to protect the secret of the genuine will and killed Leslie Firrell, who was involved in the forgery plot. Drecker had even staged a clumsy accident with a flower vase during the Halloween party to explain why she was wet after drowning Joyce.

The truth about Miranda's parentage is also revealed: Judith Butler is the mother of Garfield's daughter, having fled him years earlier due to his cold and sociopathic nature. Garfield, driven by his obsession with beauty and sacrifice, attempts to complete a ritual at the stone circle but ultimately dies by his own hand.

title: 帷幕
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\128-帷幕-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Captain Arthur Hastings travels to Styles Manor, a luxury hotel in the Essex countryside, following an invitation from his old friend, Hercule Poirot. Poirot, who has returned from Egypt suffering from worsening arthritis, is staying at the manor for treatment. Hastings is accompanied by his daughter, Judith, an intelligent science graduate working for Dr. John Franklin, a researcher specializing in tropical diseases and alkaloids.

Upon arrival, Hastings finds Poirot physically diminished—thin, shriveled, and confined to a wheelchair—though the detective remains mentally sharp. Poirot reveals his true motive for being at Styles: he is hunting a mysterious individual known as "X." Poirot presents Hastings with a summary of five seemingly unrelated murder cases where a common thread exists: a mysterious person, "X," was present near the victims despite having no motive and being hundreds of miles away during the actual crimes. Poirot predicts that "X" is currently at Styles Manor and that a murder is imminent.

As the atmosphere at the manor grows tense, various interpersonal conflicts emerge. Judith expresses disdain for her stepmother, Barbara Franklin, whom she views as a manipulative woman who uses her illness to control her husband. Meanwhile, Hastings becomes increasingly suspicious of Captain Alton, a married man who appears to be pursuing Judith. Driven by a desire to protect his daughter, Hastings attempts to sabotage Alton by replacing his sleeping pills with aspirin, intending to spike a drink with a lethal dose. However, Hastings is overcome by remorse and confesses his plan to Poirot, who rebukes him for his foolishness.

The tension culminates in the death of Barbara Franklin. She is found dead after consuming poison, and a coroner's inquest rules it a suicide due to her depression. Poirot, however, privately informs Hastings that he believes it was premeditated murder. Shortly thereafter, Stephen Norton is found dead in his locked bedroom with a gunshot wound to the forehead. Although the death is ruled a suicide, Poirot is convinced it was murder. Following these events, Poirot dies of a heart attack, leaving Hastings to act as the executor of his will.

In a series of posthumous revelations through a manuscript and letters, the truth is unveiled. Poirot reveals that he had been feigning paralysis to observe the guests and that he had witnessed Hastings' attempt to poison Alton, which he prevented. He also clarifies the truth behind Barbara Franklin's death: she had intended to kill her husband, John, to marry the wealthy Boyd Carrington, but through a series of accidental switches during a coffee service, she consumed the poison intended for her husband. Poirot allowed the death to be ruled a suicide to protect the Franklins.

The most profound revelation concerns the identity of "X." Poirot explains that "X" was not a direct murderer, but a psychological catalyst. The killer was Stephen Norton, a sadist who used his compassionate persona to manipulate the hidden, violent impulses of others, driving them to commit crimes while he remained above suspicion. Norton had been manipulating the residents of Styles to stir conflict, including targeting the relationship between Judith and Alton, and even attempting to manipulate Hastings himself by feeding him false information to make him view Alton as a scoundrel.

Finally, Poirot confesses to the murder of Stephen Norton. To prevent Norton from further manipulating the truth and causing more destruction, Poirot orchestrated a plan to kill him. Poirot drugged Norton with sleeping pills, disguised himself as Norton using a wig, a false beard, and Norton's pajamas, and shot him in his room to make the death appear to be a suicide. Poirot justifies this act as a necessary means to save innocent lives, leaving Hastings with the task of uncovering the final pieces of the puzzle.

title: 谋杀启事
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\134-谋杀启事-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In the village of Chipping Clegg Horn, a series of peculiar newspaper advertisements announce that a murder is scheduled to take place on Friday, October 29th, at 6:30 PM. While most residents, including Colonel and Laura Eastbrook, dismiss the notices as a murder mystery game, others are unsettled. Miss Letitia Blacklock, a resident of a large Victorian house, initially views the notice as a tasteless joke, though her friend Dora Bunner finds it frightening.

On the evening of the scheduled "murder," several neighbors gather at the Blacklock residence, including the Eastbrooks, the Sweetnams, Mrs. Harmon, and the Simmons siblings, Patrick and Julia. As the clock chimes 6:15, the lights suddenly go out. In the darkness, a man with a hoarse voice enters, brandishing a flashlight and firing a revolver. Amidst the chaos, a third shot is fired, and a body is discovered. When the lights are restored, the group finds a man in a black hooded cloak and mask dead on the floor. The man is identified as Rudolf Scherz, a Swiss national and career criminal. Miss Blacklock is found with blood trickling from her face, which she claims is a mere graze from a bullet.

Inspector Corradino and Chief Constable Fletcher lead the investigation. They discover that Scherz had a criminal history of forgery and theft and had been working at the local Royal Spa Hotel. Evidence suggests the crime scene was staged; a door had been oiled to allow for a silent exit, and the lighting system had been tampered with. Suspicions fall on various residents, including the household cook, Micky, a foreign refugee, and the Simmons siblings. It is revealed that "Julia Simmons" is actually Emma, one of the descendants of Sonia Goddard—the sister of the late millionaire Randall Goddard. Emma and her relative "Pip" (later identified as Philippa) had been attempting to secure their inheritance by targeting the Blacklock estate.

The investigation takes a darker turn when Dora Bunner dies of an anesthetic overdose, appearing to have taken the wrong aspirin. Shortly thereafter, Miss Muggleton is found strangled in her garden. Miss Marple, an astute observer, begins to piece together the truth. She realizes that the "murder" was not a game or a robbery gone wrong, but a meticulously planned event orchestrated by the homeowner herself.

Miss Marple reveals that the woman known as Letitia Blacklock is actually her sister, Charlotte Blacklock. Years earlier, Charlotte, who suffered from a disfiguring thyroid condition, had switched identities with the more "stable" Letitia. Charlotte had lived as Letitia to secure the inheritance from the Goddard family, which she believed she deserved after her sister's death. To facilitate her plan, Charlotte recruited Scherz to participate in a staged robbery, intending to make his death look like an accident during a prank. She had stolen a revolver from Colonel Eastbrook and sabotaged the house's electricity to create the necessary darkness.

Charlotte’s motive for the staged robbery was to protect Philippa (the real "Pip"), whom she viewed as a daughter figure, by providing a scapegoat in Scherz. However, Charlotte's deception was threatened by Dora Bunner, whose increasing confusion and memory of Charlotte's true identity made her a liability. Charlotte killed Dora as an "act of mercy" to protect her own secret.

To catch her, Miss Marple and Chief Constable Fletcher orchestrated a trap. They manipulated Micky into providing false testimony to rattle Charlotte, and Miss Marple used her ability to mimic Dora Bunner's voice to break Charlotte's composure. Charlotte is arrested, and the truth of the identity swap and the subsequent murders is brought to light. In the aftermath, Edmund and Philippa marry, and the village returns to its quiet, albeit complicated, life.

title: 命案目睹记
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\136-命案目睹记-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In December, Mrs. Elizabeth McGillicuddy, a traveler from northern England, witnessed a brutal murder while riding a train. Looking through the window of a passing first-class carriage, she saw a tall, dark-haired man strangling a blonde woman in a grayish-white fur coat. When she attempted to report the crime, the conductor was skeptical, suggesting she might have been dreaming or influenced by a violent magazine article. Fearing her claims would be dismissed, Mrs. McGillicuddy took precautions by sending a note to the stationmaster's office before continuing her journey.

Seeking assistance, Mrs. McGillicuddy traveled to St. Mary Mead to meet Jane Marple. Miss Marple remained unconvinced that the event was a delusion and theorized that the killer might have disposed of the body from the train near a sharp bend in the tracks. After a police investigation led by Inspector Cornish found no body, no hospital records, and no evidence of a crime, Miss Marple remained convinced that a clever killer was involved. She hypothesized that the murderer might have hidden the body in a corner seat to make the victim appear asleep before fleeing the train.

To investigate further, Miss Marple recruited Lucy Ashebrook, a highly capable woman, to pose as a housekeeper at Rutherford Hall, a large estate situated near a railway embankment. Miss Marple suspected the killer had used the estate as an "island" to hide the crime. While working at the manor, Lucy explored the grounds and discovered broken thorny scrub, a piece of light brown fur, and a cheap porcelain powder box among the weeds near the railway embankment. These findings supported Miss Marple’s theory that a body had been moved from the train onto the embankment.

While searching near the estate's outbuildings, Lucy discovered a woman's body wearing a fur coat inside a stone sarcophagus in a nearby warehouse. The deceased was identified as Anna Stravinsky, a dancer from a traveling ballet troupe. The discovery triggered a police investigation led by Inspector Bacon and Inspector Claddock. The investigation uncovered complex family dynamics at Rutherford Hall, where the elderly Mr. Crickenshaw lived with his children: Emma, Cedric, Harold, and Alfred. The family's substantial wealth was held in a trust, creating significant financial motives for the various heirs.

Amidst the investigation, a woman named Martina Dubois arrived, claiming to be the widow of the deceased Edmund Crickenshaw and the mother of his son. However, she revealed she was not the woman in the coffin, suggesting the killer used the "Martina" identity as a fraudulent scheme to extort the family. The investigation into the victim's identity revealed that Anna Stravinsky had disappeared around the time of the murder.

The situation escalated when a mass poisoning occurred at the manor. After a dinner prepared by Lucy, several family members, including Emma, Harold, Alfred, and Cedric, fell ill. Dr. Campbell, the family physician, determined that the meal was laced with arsenic, intended to mimic mushroom poisoning. During the chaos, Alfred died after being given tea that contained poison. Shortly thereafter, Harold also died after taking pills that were actually disguised aconite, intended to look like his regular medication.

Miss Marple, observing the patterns of greed and deception, realized the killer was someone with access to the household and a motive to consolidate the Crickenshaw fortune. She suspected the killer was manipulating the family's medical and financial situations. She noted that the deaths of Alfred and Harold would increase the inheritance for the remaining heirs.

In a final confrontation at Rutherford Hall, Miss Marple orchestrated a trap. During afternoon tea, Mrs. McGillicuddy recognized Dr. Campbell as the man she had seen on the train. Miss Marple seized the moment, using a bluff by claiming Mrs. McGillicuddy was an eyewitness who had seen the killer's face. This forced Campbell to react. Miss Marple revealed that Dr. Campbell was the murderer. He had killed his wife, Anna Stravinsky, to marry the wealthy Emma without the risk of bigamy. He had hidden the body in the warehouse and attempted to poison the family with arsenic and aconite to secure the inheritance. The investigation concluded with the exposure of Campbell's long-term deception and his violent pursuit of wealth.

title: 破镜谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\137-破镜谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In the village of Saint Mary Mead, Miss Jane Marple observes the shifting character of her community, noting the tension between traditional life and modern developments. While managing her household and her companion, the patronizing Miss Knight, Miss Marple remains deeply attuned to the nuances of human nature. Her observations extend to a local residential development where she witnesses a young woman, Lily, being neglected by her partner, Harry, prompting Miss Marple to warn her against such a marriage.

The tranquility of the village is shattered when Heather Badcock, the local secretary for the St. John Ambulance, dies suddenly during a high-profile fundraising gala held at Gossington Hall. The event was hosted by the famous film star Marina Gregg and her husband, Jason Rad. During the reception, Heather experienced a sudden collapse and died shortly thereafter. An autopsy reveals that she died from an overdose of a benzodiazepine-related drug, specifically a brand called Camon. Witnesses, including Mrs. Bantry, recall that during the event, Marina Gregg appeared to freeze in a state of profound horror while looking at a painting of a Madonna.

As the investigation unfolds, Detective Chief Inspector Demott Craddock and other investigators examine various suspects. The circumstances are bizarre: Heather had been drinking a cocktail when a commotion occurred, and Marina Gregg had subsequently handed Heather her own glass after a spill. The police consider whether Heather was the intended target or if the poisoning was an accident. Suspicion falls on several individuals, including Marina’s secretary, Ella Jelinsky, and her husband, Jason Rad. The investigation is further complicated by a series of threatening letters sent to Marina, suggesting an assassin is targeting her.

The death of Heather Badcock triggers a chain of further tragedies. Ella Jelinsky dies after inhaling what she believes to be a nasal spray, but which is actually cyanide. Shortly thereafter, the Italian butler, Giuseppe, is shot and killed. These deaths increase Marina Gregg's paranoia, and she becomes convinced that someone is actively trying to kill her. Jason Rad attempts to protect her by remaining silent about his suspicions of an attempted murder, fearing that the truth would destroy her fragile mental state.

Miss Marple, utilizing her understanding of human behavior and her study of celebrity gossip, begins to piece together the truth. She focuses on the history of Marina Gregg’s life, including her past marriages and her adopted children. She also investigates the specific details of the gala, such as the illnesses present among the guests. Miss Marple discovers a crucial link: years earlier, during a chance encounter in Bermuda, a young Heather Badcock had attended a party while suffering from measles. In doing so, she had unknowingly infected a pregnant Marina Gregg, leading to the birth of a child with severe physical and mental disabilities.

Miss Marple confronts Jason Rad and the investigators at the scene of the crime. She reveals that the motive for the murder was not greed or professional jealousy, but a long-simmering, impulsive act of revenge. When Heather Badcock encountered Marina Gregg again at the gala and boasted about her "bravery" in attending the previous event while sick, Marina acted. Marina had already placed a lethal dose of camphor in her own drink. When a spill occurred, Marina manipulated the situation so that Heather would consume the poisoned liquid. To deflect suspicion, Marina had staged the threatening letters and the apparent attempts on her own life to frame herself as the intended victim.

The investigation concludes with the revelation that Jason Rad had been complicit in the cover-up, protecting his wife from the consequences of her actions. As the truth is laid bare, Miss Marple finds Marina Gregg dead in her bed, her death potentially a result of a self-administered overdose or further foul play, leaving the finality of the star's suffering unresolved.

title: 沉睡谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\140-沉睡谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Gwendraith (Gwendolyn) Ridd, a twenty-one-year-old orphan from New Zealand, travels to England to find a permanent home to share with her husband, Jervis (Jules). While exploring the south coast, she discovers and purchases a white Victorian villa in Dartmouth known as Mountain Slope Villa (or St. Catherine’s Villa). As she begins renovations, Gwendolyn experiences inexplicable sensations of fear and a strange sense of familiarity with the house’s layout and wallpaper. These experiences, coupled with a terrifying vision of a strangled woman named Helen with a green face and non-human hands, lead her to seek counsel from her cousin Joan West and the novelist Raymond West in Chelsea.

During a visit to Chelsea, Gwendolyn meets the astute Miss Marple. Gwendolyn confesses her fears of losing her mind, describing the vivid, traumatic visions. Miss Marple suggests that these are not supernatural hauntings but rather repressed childhood memories. Gwendolyn recalls being born in India and having fragmented memories of a nanny named Southie and a man in a military uniform. Miss Marple’s theory is confirmed when Gwendolyn’s Aunt Alison sends a letter revealing that Gwendolyn’s father, Colonel Kelvin Hardy, had indeed sent her to England to live with relatives after a series of family upheavals.

Driven by a need to uncover her past, Gwendolyn and Jervis begin an investigation into her father’s history. They discover that Kelvin Hardy had lived in the very villa Gwendolyn now owns. They learn that Kelvin’s second wife, Helen Kennedy, had disappeared eighteen years prior. Dr. Kennedy, Helen’s brother, informs them that Kelvin had suffered a mental breakdown, believing he had strangled Helen, though he claimed to have found only a note from her stating she was leaving him. Kelvin eventually died in a mental institution, convinced of his own guilt.

The investigation intensifies when Lily Kimball, a former maid at the villa who had long suspected foul play, is found strangled in a wooded area. Her death mirrors the circumstances of Helen’s disappearance, suggesting that the old mystery is being reopened by modern violence. Gwendolyn and Jervis identify several potential suspects from the past: the obsessive lawyer Walter Feen, the unstable Jackie Averycliff, or Colonel Richard Erskine, who had a romantic connection to Helen.

As the tension mounts, Gwendolyn experiences a moment of psychological terror when she perceives Dr. Kennedy as a threat, fearing he intends to kill her to protect his secrets. However, Miss Marple intervenes, and the truth is finally brought to light.

Miss Marple reveals that the true killer was Dr. Kennedy. Driven by a sinister, possessive love for his half-sister, Helen, Kennedy had gone insane when she married Kelvin Hardy. He strangled Helen in the hallway of the villa and orchestrated a complex deception to frame Kelvin. He manipulated Kelvin into believing he had committed the murder, forged Helen’s handwriting in letters to simulate her departure, and staged the scene to make it appear as though she had eloped. To maintain this lie, Kennedy murdered Lily Kimball after she became aware of the truth through a childhood witness named Leonie. Kennedy also attempted to poison Gwendolyn with brandy to silence her. Ultimately, Kennedy confessed to his crimes before his death, resolving the long-dormant murder case and providing Gwendolyn with the truth about her father and her stepmother.

title: 烟囱别墅之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\142-烟囱别墅之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Anthony Ked, a quick-witted tour guide in Bravaio, is approached by his old friend Jimmy McGregor with a high-stakes mission. Jimmy tasks Anthony with delivering the memoirs of Count Stapfitch, a powerful former Prime Minister of Hesoslovakia, to a London publisher for a £1,000 reward. The manuscript is politically sensitive, potentially exposing the vices of the House of Paulovich and jeopardizing the restoration of the Heslovich monarchy. Additionally, Anthony agrees to help Jimmy return a package of blackmail letters to a woman associated with "Chimney Cottage," a prestigious British mansion.

As Anthony travels to England using the alias Jimmy McGregor, the political stakes escalate. George Romax, working to secure oil drilling rights in Hesoslovakia for a British company, pressures the ninth Earl of Carrington to host a hunting party at Chimney House to provide cover for these dealings. The party is intended to support the ascension of Prince Michael O'Paulwich. Meanwhile, Virginia Rue, a charming widow, is recruited by Romax to use her social influence to manage McGregor.

In London, Anthony is targeted by various factions. Baron Lothair Rottschläger, representing the Heslovian royal court, offers him bribes to abandon the manuscript, but Anthony refuses. Anthony is also attacked by a member of the Red Hand Party and later by an Italian waiter named Giuseppe, who steals the blackmail letters from Anthony's room. Anthony eventually secures the real manuscript by providing a fake to his publisher and hiding the original.

The plot converges at Chimney House, where a murder occurs. Prince Michael is found shot dead in the banquet hall. The investigation, led by local Inspector Bagshaw and later Chief Constable Bartle, is complicated by several factors: a missing weapon, muddy footprints near a window, and the presence of various suspicious guests, including the American Hiram Fisk and the French detective Monsieur Le Mon. Anthony, arriving at the estate to clear his name after being implicated by footprints, begins his own investigation.

Anthony discovers that the victim, thought to be "Count Stanislaus," was actually Prince Michael. He suspects the murder is linked to the memoirs and a legendary jewel, the Light Mountain Diamond. Through a series of maneuvers, including a staged burglary in the council chamber and a tense confrontation in the library, Anthony exposes the truth. He reveals that the woman found dead in the library was an actress impersonating a governess named Miss White. This imposter had been searching for the diamond and had killed Prince Michael after he recognized her. She had also attempted to frame others by planting a gun in the suitcase of a financier, Mr. Esakistan.

Anthony further reveals that the "imposter" Le Mon was actually a French secret agent. He also clarifies that the diamond was not in the library as he had previously suggested to lure the killer, but was hidden in the garden near a rose bush named Richmond, following a code found in the Count's memoirs.

In the final resolution, Anthony reveals his true identity: he is Prince Nikolai, the rightful heir to the Heslovich throne. He had been using aliases to navigate the dangerous political landscape. He also reveals that he has married Virginia Rue. Anthony accepts his role as king to secure the throne and stabilize the region's oil interests, much to the relief of the Baron and Mr. Esakistan. He tasks Jimmy McGregor with delivering the real, politically dull memoirs to a secure address in London. Anthony and Virginia prepare to depart for Hesoslovakia to begin their reign.

title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
At the Chimney House, known as Smokestack Villa, a group of residents and guests live under the strict, punctual eye of the landlady, Mrs. Cutt. Among them is Gerry Wade, a young man whose sudden death becomes the catalyst for a complex mystery. A group of friends, including Jimmy Sycamore, Bill Everly, and Ronnie DeVille, decide to play a prank on the oversleeping Gerry by hiding several loud alarm clocks in his room. They enlist the serious Rupert Bettman to help place the clocks.

The prank goes awry when Gerry is found dead in his bed from an overdose of chloral hydrate. While the death is initially suspected to be a suicide, Ronnie DeVille remains convinced of foul play. Shortly after, Ronnie is shot and killed, leaving behind a cryptic message regarding a "seven-faced clock" and a directive to "tell Jimmy Sycamore."

Eileen Bondel, the daughter of Lord Kettlemere, becomes drawn into the investigation. She discovers an unfinished letter from Gerry Wade mentioning a "seven clock thing" and expressing extreme fatigue. Bondel’s investigation leads her to the Seven Dials Club, a smoke-filled basement establishment. By infiltrating the club and hiding in a wardrobe, she witnesses a secret meeting of individuals wearing hourglass masks. The group discusses international interests, a valuable invention by a German named Hel Eberhard, and the need to silence suspicious individuals.

The mystery deepens during a gala at the Two-Footed Dragon Church. An attempted theft of political documents occurs, resulting in a struggle where Jimmy Sycamore is wounded by a gunshot. Amidst the chaos, Bondel realizes that many of the events are staged. She eventually discovers that the "Seven Clocks" is not a criminal organization, but a secret intelligence group of amateur spies founded by the late Gerry Wade to protect national interests and catch high-level thieves. The members include Detective Bartholomew (the leader), Mosgolovsky, Bill Everly, and the Countess Razky (who is actually an actress named Barbara Saint-Moore).

Detective Bartholomew reveals the truth behind the deaths. Gerry Wade was murdered by Jimmy Sycamore, who had poisoned his drink. Ronnie DeVille had deduced Sycamore's guilt and was attempting to use the alarm clock prank to signal the truth, but he was eliminated by Sycamore. Sycamore had also staged the shooting at the church to create an alibi; he had shot himself in the arm with a Mauser pistol and thrown it out a window to simulate a struggle with an intruder. He had even used a burnt glove to attempt to hide evidence of his left-handedness, as he was ambidextrous.

The final conspiracy involved Sycamore attempting to poison Bill Everly to cover his tracks, but Everly, acting as a decoy for the spy group, feigned unconsciousness. Sycamore’s true nature as a ruthless criminal is exposed, and he is sentenced to be hanged. In the aftermath, Bondel rejects a proposal from the persistent George Lomax and decides to marry Bill Everly.

title: 控方证人
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\145-控方证人-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
The novel is a collection of interconnected narratives exploring psychic phenomena, mental instability, and the thin line between the supernatural and the criminal.

The story begins with a narrator traveling to Cornwall, where he learns of a nun, Sister Mary Angelique, rumored to possess the power to summon lightning. In the village, Dr. Ross, a physician interested in studying her alleged psychic abilities, arranges for her to live with a nurse. During an investigation, a woman named Mary-Annichelle recounts visions of a "Crystal House" and a "death hound." Dr. Ross, theorizing that supernatural events may be misunderstood natural laws, conducts hypnotic experiments on Sister Mary Angelique using crystals. He identifies various "wonders" corresponding to colors and symbols, including a sixth wonder representing destructive power. The narrator later discovers Dr. Ross’s diary, which reveals an obsession with this sixth miracle and his decision to administer continuous morphine to the nun. Following a landslide that destroys Dr. Ross’s house—leaving wreckage resembling a giant dog—the doctor, the nurse, and Sister Mary Angelique all perish.

The narrative shifts to a dinner party where the psychiatrist Sir Arlington West discusses intuition and the subconscious. His nephew, Dermot, experiences a "red signal" of intense danger, realizing the group is being observed by the unsettling Sir Arlington. During a séance led by a medium named Mrs. Thompson, a sinister voice warns Dermot of impending blood. Sir Arlington later confronts Dermot, claiming that Claire Trent, a woman Dermot loves, is mentally ill and must be restrained. This leads to a confrontation where Dermot vows to protect Claire. Later, at a gallery, Claire confesses her love for Dermot but urges him to leave due to a sense of shame.

The plot takes a dark turn when Dermot returns home to find a revolver planted in his apartment to frame him for the murder of Sir Arlington. Realizing he is being set up, Dermot escapes and meets Jack Trent, who reveals a shocking truth: Trent himself killed Sir Arlington and planted the weapon to frame Dermot. Trent also confesses a sinister obsession with Claire. Before he can act further, Claire shoots and kills Trent.

A separate thread follows the case of Félicité Bauer, a girl with multiple distinct personalities. Her history is tied to Annette Lavelle, a dominant and ambitious girl who used hypnosis to manipulate Félicité. After Annette dies of tuberculosis, Félicité undergoes a mental breakdown, adopting different personas, including a talented pianist. Her life ends in a mysterious death where she appears to have strangled herself with superhuman strength.

In another segment, Dicky Carpenter recounts warnings from a mysterious woman in a red scarf, Alice Thurston Howarth, who possesses "second sight." After Dicky dies from anesthesia, the narrative explores the connection between the "gift" of sight and the environment. Jack Hattersley, hearing recurring screams of "Murder!" at a specific time and location, investigates a villa called Heather House. Alongside a "soul doctor" named Lavinton and a woman named Felice, Jack conducts a séance using a Ming Dynasty porcelain jar. The experience ends in confusion, with Lavinton claiming the supernatural elements were merely scientific deceptions.

The focus then moves to the Walden estate, where Dr. Castles investigates Arthur Carmichael, who has regressed to a primitive, non-human state. His stepmother, Lady Cammichael, is suspected of trying to render him an incurable idiot to secure his inheritance. Following a series of unsettling events involving a spectral gray cat and a missing book on human-to-animal transformation, Lady Cammichael is found dead with claw marks on her throat. Arthur is subsequently poisoned with cyanide, and Dr. Castles concludes that Lady Cammichael died of shock, suggesting a "one life for another" exchange.

The final segments involve various individuals facing psychological or physical crises. Sylas Hammel, a man plagued by visions of spiritual "wings" and the heavy pull of materialism, decides to donate his fortune to the poor. In his attempt to achieve spiritual freedom, he sacrifices himself to save a man on a metro platform. In France, a medium named Simonne undergoes a terrifying séance for a grieving mother, Madame Ix. During the session, a manifestation of a child appears, but the encounter ends in a gruesome scene where the mother is found dead and seemingly shrunken.

The novel concludes with the investigation of the Dinsmoor family. Mortimer Cleveland, a researcher, seeks shelter in their home and notices signs of distress, including "SOS" messages written in dust. He deduces that the family is being poisoned with arsenic. He reveals that Mr. Dinsmoor has been intentionally poisoning his adopted daughter, Charlotte, to ensure that his biological daughter, Madgeleine, inherits a large fortune.

title: 怪屋
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\150-怪屋-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
During the end of the war in Egypt, Charles meets Sophia Leontidis, an efficient manager at a Foreign Ministry outpost. They develop a close friendship, and Charles intends to marry her upon his return to England. Sophia reveals she lives in a "weird little cottage" in Swindon with her extended family, including her wealthy Greek grandfather, Aristide Leonidis. Two years later, Charles returns to England and reads an obituary announcing Aristide's sudden death at his home in Swinley. When Charles meets Sophia, he senses unease regarding the circumstances of the death and her calm demeanor.

Charles proposes marriage, but Sophia hesitates, revealing her suspicion that Aristide was murdered. She notes that a doctor refused to issue a death certificate and warns Charles that they are being watched. Charles seeks advice from his father, an assistant to the Commissioner at Scotland Yard, who suggests that Charles should only marry Sophia if the killer is identified. Inspector Taverner arrives to investigate, confirming that Aristide was poisoned, likely with aconitine from his own eyedrops. The investigation focuses on the Leonidis household, a distorted, eleven-sided manor in Swinley. Suspects include Aristide's young widow, Brenda, and her lover, Lawrence Brown, a tutor to the grandchildren.

Charles enters the household to gather information, a move Sophia agrees to so they can find the truth. Sophia describes her family as "cold-blooded," characterizing members like her father, Philip, and her mother, Magda, as emotionally distant. Aunt Edith de Havilland, who has lived at the estate for forty years, also provides insight into the family's complex dynamics. The investigation reveals that while many family members are financially independent, Roger Leonidis, Sophia's uncle, is facing bankruptcy with his wife, Clementine. This provides a potential motive for Roger to kill his father for inheritance, though Roger produces a letter from Aristide suggesting the old man intended to help his business.

The case becomes more complicated when a dispute arises regarding Aristide's will. While the family claims a signed will exists, the lawyer, Mr. Gaskill, discovers that the document in the safe is actually an unsigned version, suggesting the original signed will was switched. Amidst this tension, Josephine, a young girl with a "monster-like" appearance, begins acting as a self-appointed detective, eavesdropping on the adults.

The situation turns violent when Josephine is found injured by a marble doorstop that had been set as a trap in a laundry room. During the chaos, incriminating love letters between Brenda and Lawrence are discovered in a water tank room, suggesting a conspiracy. Based on these letters, the police arrest Brenda and Lawrence for the poisoning of Aristide. However, Charles remains skeptical, suspecting that the true killer is still in the house. His suspicions are heightened when the household's nanny dies after accidentally drinking poisoned hot chocolate intended for Josephine.

As the investigation intensifies, Charles and his father evaluate the family members, considering Philip's resentment, Clementine's ruthlessness, and the possibility of an intruder. Charles realizes that the intruder who ransacked Josephine's room was likely searching for her black notebook, which contains her "intelligence findings."

The mystery reaches a tragic conclusion following a car crash that kills Edith de Havilland and Josephine. Charles discovers a confession letter from Edith, in which she claims responsibility for the murders of Aristide and the nanny to protect Brenda and Lawrence. However, Charles also finds Josephine's missing black notebook. The notebook reveals the truth: Josephine was the killer. Driven by a desire for independence and resentment toward her family, she had orchestrated the poisoning of her grandfather, the poisoning of the nanny, and the staged attack on herself using the doorstop. Edith had discovered the notebook and, out of a misguided sense of love, had attempted to take the blame to save the child. Charles informs his father of the truth and proposes marriage to Sophia, planning to start a new life away from the "house of deformities."

title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
The narrative unfolds through several interconnected threads of mystery, deception, and retribution.

On Majorca, Mr. Parker Pyne observes a growing tension between Mrs. Adela Chester and her son, Basil. Mrs. Chester, a rigid conservative, disapproves of Basil’s relationship with the modern Betty Gregg. Pyne intervenes, eventually discovering that the glamorous woman Basil has become infatuated with, Deloris Lamorna, is actually an actress named Madeline Salieri. Pyne orchestrates Deloris's departure from the island to break Basil's infatuation, which ultimately leads to a reconciliation between Basil, Betty, and his mother.

At Litcham Manor, Hercule Poirot investigates the death of Hubert Litcham Rock. Although the room is locked and a suicide note is present, Poirot rejects the suicide verdict. He discovers that the secretary, Jeffrey Keene, murdered Rock with a silencer and staged the scene to look like a suicide. Keene attempted to create a false timeline by firing a pistol from a window, but he was betrayed by footprints in a flower bed and a misplaced silk rosebud. Poirot reveals that Keene killed the victim earlier and used the gunshot to mimic a different time of death.

In a separate investigation, Barton Russell hosts a dinner to commemorate the death of his wife, Eileen. During the meal, Polly Weatherby is poisoned with cyanide. Poirot reveals that Russell himself was the killer; he poisoned Polly to secure her inheritance and attempted to frame Stephen Carter by planting a box of cyanide in his pocket. Polly had suspected Russell's scheme and had even sent a forged, mysterious message to attract Poirot's attention.

Mr. Satterthwaite encounters the enigmatic Mr. Quinn, who provides cryptic insights into the nature of death and destiny. This leads Satterthwaite to the Doveton Kingsburn estate, owned by his friend Tom Addison. At a tea party hosted by Tom, a potential poisoning is narrowly averted when Satterthwaite realizes that Beryl Gillett has swapped teacups to exploit the color blindness of Timothy, a young man at the gathering. Satterthwaite deduces that Beryl intended to kill Timothy to secure a fortune for her son, Roland.

Another theft occurs involving the "Morning Star" diamond during a dinner hosted by Mr. Bohns. Yvonne, a guest, executes a plan to steal the gem, but the theft is revealed to be a sophisticated deception carried out by the Amalfi family. They used play-doh and a fake diamond to facilitate the crime, with the real stone being hidden via a clever swap.

A separate thread follows Joyce Lambert, a woman struggling with extreme poverty and the loss of her beloved fox terrier, Trey. After her attempts to find stability through marriage to Arthur Harley fail, she accepts a job caring for an elderly aunt and child, discovering that her employer, Mr. Alabi, was the kind man who helped her when Trey was injured.

The final and most complex thread centers on Monkswell Manor, a guesthouse run by Molly and Jules Davis. The household is isolated by a blizzard and becomes the site of a targeted killing. A woman named Morine Gregg (alias Madame Lyon) has been murdered in London, and a notebook titled "Three Blind Mice" links the crime to a 1940 incident at Longridge Farm, where three children were involved in a tragedy. The residents of the manor—including the domineering Mrs. Boyle, the military man Major Metcalf, and the deserter Christopher Lane—all become suspects.

After Mrs. Boyle is strangled in her room, a man claiming to be Constable Trotter arrives to conduct an experiment to identify a liar among the guests. The tension peaks when the man reveals himself to be Jim, a survivor of the Longridge Farm abuse, who holds Molly at gunpoint for revenge. He is subdued by Major Metcalf, who is revealed to be working undercover with the real Detective Inspector Taner. The mystery concludes with the resolution of the Longridge Farm vendetta and the reconciliation of Molly and Jules, who realize they had both secretly traveled to London to buy anniversary gifts for one another.

title: ABC谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\198-ABC谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
In June 1935, Captain Arthur Hastings returns to London and visits Hercule Poirot at his modern service apartment. Their meeting is interrupted by a typed letter signed "ABC," which challenges Poirot to solve a mystery and warns him to "watch out for Andover" on the twenty-first of the month. While Hastings dismisses the letter as a prank, Poirot is deeply unsettled.

On the twenty-first, an elderly tobacconist named Mrs. Asher is killed by a blow to the head in Andover. An ABC train timetable is found open at the scene. The primary suspect is her estranged, alcoholic husband, Franz Ascher, but Poirot notes the handwriting on the ABC letter is too clear for the paralyzed Franz. During his investigation, Poirot discovers that the killer likely uses high-quality paper and a typewriter, suggesting a person driven by a desire for attention. He also notes that the killer may have been influenced by a bottle of White Horse whisky, as a subsequent letter was misaddressed to "White Horse Apartments" instead of his actual residence, "White Harbor Apartments."

A second letter arrives, announcing that the "game" is beginning and directing attention to Bexhill-on-Sea on the twenty-fifth. On that date, a waitress named Elizabeth Barnard is found strangled on Bexhill Beach with her own woven belt. An ABC booklet is found near her body. Poirot interviews the deceased's sister, Megan Barnard, and her fiancé, Donald Fraser, learning that Elizabeth had a volatile relationship with Fraser, who was prone to jealousy. Despite the alphabetical pattern, Poirot remains puzzled by the lack of a consistent demographic profile among the victims.

A third letter arrives, mocking Poirot and announcing a crime in Churston on the thirtieth. In Churston, Sir Clement Clarke, a throat specialist, is found dead from a blow to the head, with an ABC timetable placed on his body. His brother, Franklin Clarke, expresses skepticism about the serial killer theory and suggests the deaths might be unrelated. During the investigation, Tola Grey, Sir Clement’s secretary, expresses intense fear regarding the "C" in the ABC sequence.

The investigation intensifies as a fourth letter predicts a crime in Doncaster on September 11th. Poirot observes a pattern: a man selling stockings has been seen near the crime scenes. This leads the police to focus on Alexander Bonaparte Cast, a timid, epileptic door-to-door stocking salesman. Cast’s initials match the "ABC" signature, and he is found in possession of ABC timetables and a knife. During a chaotic event at a cinema in Doncaster, a man named George Erskine is stabbed; Cast is seen washing blood from his sleeve, and a knife is found in his pocket. Cast is eventually apprehended, and while he confesses to being a killer, he is confused about his own actions due to his epilepsy and memory gaps.

While the police prepare to prosecute Cast, Poirot remains unsatisfied. He realizes that the "ABC" serial killer pattern was a sophisticated ruse. He forms a special task force consisting of the victims' friends and relatives to observe them. During a final confrontation, Poirot reveals that the true killer is Franklin Clarke.

Franklin orchestrated the entire alphabetical sequence to serve as a distraction for a single, premeditated motive: the inheritance of his brother Sir Clement’s estate. Franklin feared that if Sir Clement married his secretary, Tola Grey, he would lose his future wealth. Franklin manipulated the unstable and epileptic Cast to act as his scapegoat, providing him with the typewriter and instructions to mimic a serial killer. Franklin even staged the cinema murder to frame Cast by planting a knife on him and wiping blood on Cast's sleeve. Poirot reveals that the fingerprint evidence used to break Franklin's composure was a bluff, but the logic of the "ABC" pattern could only be explained by a mastermind using the chaos to mask a personal crime. Franklin attempts suicide but is arrested by Scotland Yard.

title: 牙医谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\203-牙医谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot is drawn into a complex web of murder and deception following the death of Mr. Morley, a successful dentist at 58 Charlotte Queen Street. While the official investigation led by Inspector Japp concludes that Morley committed suicide due to a medical error involving a lethal overdose of anesthetic, Poirot remains skeptical. The death of Morley occurs shortly after the death of a "fat foreigner" named Mr. Ambrosio, a man with ties to espionage and blackmail.

The investigation is complicated by the disappearance of Miss Mabel Sainsbury-Sil, a patient of Morley’s. When a decomposed, mutilated corpse is discovered in a fur box in an apartment belonging to a reclusive woman named Sylvia Chapman, the police mistakenly identify the victim as Mrs. Albert Chapman. This error leads to a series of false leads regarding Miss Sainsbury-Sil’s whereabouts. Meanwhile, political tensions rise as an assassination attempt is made on Alistair Brent, a powerful and wealthy banker, by an Indian student. The student is apprehended by Howard Ricks, a radical young man and patient of the late Morley.

Poirot’s suspicions deepen during a visit to Alistair Brent at his Gothic mansion. He observes strange family dynamics involving Brent’s niece, Jane Olivia, and her boyfriend, Howard Ricks. During a weekend at Brent's country estate, a second shooting attempt occurs; Frank Carter, a gardener with a criminal reputation, is caught with a revolver after shooting at Brent. Ricks intervenes to save Brent, claiming Carter is the assailant. However, Poirot realizes that the various political theories—ranging from communist conspiracies to international espionage—are merely distractions from a more personal motive.

Poirot begins to piece together the truth by analyzing physical discrepancies, such as a shoe buckle and the identity of the deceased woman. He realizes that the "Mabel Sainsbury-Sil" seen by witnesses was an imposter, and the mutilated corpse was actually the real, harmless Mabel. The imposter had been using Mabel's identity to facilitate a larger deception.

Poirot eventually confronts Alistair Brent, revealing that the banker’s entire life is built on a foundation of bigamy and fraud. Brent had been secretly married to a woman named Gerta Grant while maintaining a public persona. When the real Mabel Sainsbury-Sil discovered his secret, Brent orchestrated her murder. To silence a blackmailer named Ambrosio, Brent also murdered the dentist, Mr. Morley, and used his knowledge of dental procedures to kill Ambrosio by injecting him with a lethal dose of adrenaline and procaine. Brent had forged patient records, manipulated crime scenes, and even used his wife—a skilled actress—to impersonate others over the phone to mislead Poirot.

Brent admits to the murders of Mabel, Ambrosio, Morley, and Mrs. Chapman, justifying his actions through a utilitarian logic, claiming his reputation is essential to the stability of England. Poirot rejects this defense, accusing Brent of being driven by an excessive love for power, and ensures his arrest. In a final twist, as Poirot reflects on the case with Mr. Barnes, the retired civil servant, Barnes reveals that he is actually Albert Chapman, the man whose death and identity were central to the deception.

title: 清洁女工之死
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\209-清洁女工之死-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot is approached by Detective Inspector Spence, who is troubled by the recent conviction of James Bentley for the murder of Mrs. McKinty. Although substantial evidence—including stolen money and bloodstained clothing—led to Bentley's death sentence, Spence’s intuition suggests the shy, unremarkable man is innocent. Spence, facing reassignment, asks Poirot to investigate the case, suspecting that Mrs. McKinty’s death was not a suicide and that the true killer may have framed Bentley.

Mrs. McKinty was a solitary, thrifty widow living in Broadhurst who kept a small amount of cash hidden under a floorboard. On the night of her murder, she was struck on the head with a sharp object, and approximately thirty pounds was stolen. While the evidence against Bentley seemed irrefutable, Poirot posits that the motive must involve an extraordinary killer or a frame-up. Poirot begins his investigation by examining the victim's belongings and discovers that Mrs. McKinty had recently purchased ink and had been clipping articles from the *Sunday Comet* regarding "Tragic Women"—historical figures involved in scandals and crimes. One article detailed the case of Eva Kane, a woman who fled the country after her lover, Alfred Craig, was executed for murder. Poirot realizes Mrs. McKinty likely recognized a photograph in the newspaper that linked a local resident to this past crime and intended to use the information for profit.

As Poirot probes the community, he interviews various residents, including the Wetherby family, the Upwards, and the Carpenters. He notes several suspicious connections: Mrs. Upward, a wealthy woman living at Golden Chain Manor, appears to be hiding a significant past; Eve Carpenter, a beautiful woman engaged to a rising politician, displays agitation when the *Sunday Comet* is mentioned; and several women in the area share physical characteristics with the women in the newspaper profiles. During his travels, Poirot is even subjected to an assassination attempt when an unknown person pushes him toward the tracks at Gilchester station.

The investigation intensifies when Mrs. Upward is found murdered, strangled with her own scarf. The crime scene contains traces of expensive perfume and lipstick, which Poirot suspects are "props" planted to frame one of the women Mrs. Upward had recently summoned to her home. During his search, Poirot discovers a "sugar hammer"—a heavy, decorative brass tool—which he identifies as the likely murder weapon used in both the McKinty and Upward murders.
