Poirot gathers the suspects and reveals the truth. He explains that the murders were not committed by a woman, but by Robin Upward. Robin is not the biological son of Mrs. Upward; he is the son of the notorious Eva Kane (who had assumed the identity of Mrs. Upward to secure wealth and status). Robin had used a romanticized backstory to gain Mrs. Upward's affection and inheritance. When Mrs. McKinty discovered a photograph proving Robin's true parentage, Robin killed her to protect his social standing and his claim to the Upward fortune. He then killed his foster mother, Mrs. Upward, to ensure the secret remained buried. Robin had meticulously staged the crime scenes to implicate others, but his arrogance and the preservation of certain sentimental items, such as a photograph of his biological mother, led to his exposure.

In the aftermath, Poirot reveals that Maud Williams is actually Maud Craig, the daughter of Eva Kane. She had arrived in the village seeking revenge against Mrs. Upward, only to find her already dead. Poirot decides not to pursue charges against her. The case concludes with the revelation that the attempt on Poirot's life at the station was actually carried out by Dr. Lund, who feared Poirot was investigating his own past involvement in a poisoning case. Robin Upward is left to face trial for his crimes.

title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\219-字母袖扣谋杀案-苏菲·汉娜.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot’s investigation begins at "The Happy Coffee Shop" in London, where he encounters a distraught woman named Jane (also known as Jenny or Jean). She appears to be fleeing from someone and expresses a terrifying conviction that her death is inevitable and necessary for justice. She begs Poirot, a retired policeman, to swear that if she dies, he will not instruct his police contacts to investigate the killer. Following her sudden disappearance, Poirot learns from the cafe staff that Jane previously worked as a maid and often spoke in an unnatural, noble manner.

The investigation intensifies when Poirot’s companion, Carter, reports three chillingly organized murders at the Brawnsham Hotel. The victims—Harriet Sipper, Edith Gransbury, and Richard Nigges—were found in their locked rooms, meticulously arranged on the floor with their arms at their sides and palms down. Most strikingly, each victim had a gold cufflink engraved with the initials "PIJ" (or "PJI") placed in their mouths. Poirot realizes that Jane’s earlier warning—to "not let them talk"—was a specific instruction to prevent investigators from opening the victims' mouths and discovering the cufflinks.

Poirot and Carter investigate the hotel, noting several inconsistencies. While the victims were found in similar positions, Richard Nigges's room was unique: his window was open, his cufflink was pushed deep into his throat, and a key was found hidden behind a loose fireplace tile, suggesting he had locked the door from the inside and exited through the window. Poirot also notes that the victims had all checked in on the same day and requested room service at the same time. Through further inquiry, it is revealed that Nigges had pre-paid for the rooms and car rentals for the others, suggesting he may have been an unwitting accomplice in bringing the victims to the hotel.

The investigation expands to the village of Glebeholme, where Carter discovers a connection to a tragedy from sixteen years prior. In 1913, a local clergyman, Patrick James Ives, and his wife, Frances, died by suicide after being accused of spiritualist fraud and improper conduct. The accusations were fueled by malicious rumors spread by a maid named Jenny Hobbs and supported by Harriet Sipper, Ada Gransbury, and Richard Nigges. The village's condemnation had led to the couple's deaths, leaving "invisible wounds" on the survivors.

Returning to London, Poirot identifies the woman seen fleeing the hotel as the portrait painter Nancy Duane. While Nancy has an alibi provided by the Wallace family, Poirot suspects a deeper conspiracy. He discovers that the "impersonator" seen at the hotel—a man claiming to be Nigges—was actually Samuel Kid, a skilled mimic who helped facilitate the crime. Poirot realizes the murders were not a series of random killings, but a staged event.

Poirot eventually confronts Jane Hobbs, revealing that she and Nancy Duane had conspired to manipulate the situation. The truth is a complex web of guilt and survival: Richard Nigges, consumed by remorse for his role in the Ives' deaths, had proposed a suicide pact to Harriet, Ada, and Jenny to achieve religious atonement. However, the plan was subverted. Jenny Hobbs, driven by a desire to survive and a long-standing jealousy of Nancy Duane, manipulated the group. She convinced Nigges that Nancy was evil, while simultaneously working with Nancy to frame the others for the deaths.

The "murders" were actually a combination of planned suicides and killings. Jenny Hobbs killed Harriet Sipper and Richard Nigges to ensure the plan's progression and to protect herself. The victims were poisoned with cyanide earlier than the police believed, and the "impersonation" of Nigges at the hotel was a ruse to create a false timeline of life. The meticulously arranged bodies and the placement of the "PIJ" cufflinks were intended to make the deaths appear to be the work of a single, ritualistic killer, thereby directing suspicion toward Nancy Duane.

In a final, violent turn, Jane Hobbs stabs Mrs. Dunn during the confrontation. Poirot concludes the case by explaining that the entire sequence—the "alphabet cufflink murders"—was a manufactured tragedy designed to serve as a twisted form of justice and a mechanism for framing an innocent woman. Poirot admits to Carter that he used certain deceptions during the investigation to force him to utilize his own deductive reasoning.

title: 悬崖上的谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\241-悬崖上的谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
While playing golf with Dr. Thomas, twenty-eight-year-old Bobby Jones discovers a man dying at the bottom of a ravine near a cliff's edge. Before the man dies, he asks, "Why didn't they find Evans?" Bobby finds a photograph of a beautiful young woman in the man's pocket. The deceased is later identified as Alexander Prichard, a man who had recently returned from Siam. During the inquest, Prichard's sister, Amelia Kemm, testifies that he was in good spirits, and the jury concludes his death was an accident caused by fog. However, Bobby feels a sense of guilt because he withheld the man's final words from the Kemms.

Bobby's life becomes increasingly complicated. He receives a suspicious job offer from a company in Buenos Aires and later survives an attempt on his life when someone spikes his beer with lethal amounts of morphine while he is sleeping at a sanatorium. His childhood acquaintance, Frankie (Frances) DeWinter, begins investigating the connection between the poisoning, the strange job offer, and the death of Prichard. She theorizes that Prichard was murdered because he witnessed something, and that Bobby was targeted because he might have seen the killer's distinguishing features.

Frankie and Bobby develop a plan to infiltrate the Baskington-French family in Devon. Frankie stages a car accident to gain entry to the home of the magistrate, Mr. Baskington-French, under the guise of needing medical observation for a concussion. While there, she observes the erratic behavior of Henry Baskington-French, who appears to be a morphine addict, and meets his brother, Roger Baskington-French. Roger claims Henry receives treatment from a nearby doctor, Dr. Jasper Nicholson. Frankie becomes suspicious of Nicholson, noting his intense personality and his ownership of a deep blue Talbot, a car similar to one seen near the site of Prichard's death.

As the investigation deepens, Bobby and Frankie deduce that "Prichard" was not his real identity. They suspect a conspiracy involving the Kemm family to hide the identity of the deceased, whom they believe was actually Allen Castles, a Canadian explorer. They conclude that Castles was pushed off the cliff and that the Kemms falsely identified him to prevent his true identity from being revealed.

The situation escalates when Henry Baskington-French is found dead in his locked study from a gunshot wound to the temple. While the death is ruled a suicide, Bobby suspects Dr. Nicholson murdered him and staged the scene. Meanwhile, Moira Nicholson, the doctor's wife, approaches Bobby and Frankie in terror. She claims her husband is trying to kill her and is manipulating Henry and Roger to facilitate his control over the family.

Bobby, acting as a chauffeur, attempts to investigate the Nicholson sanatorium and encounters a terrified woman who matches the description of the girl in the photograph found on the deceased. This leads the pair to realize that the woman is Moira Nicholson. They also begin investigating the death of a millionaire, John Savage, whose will left a massive inheritance to a woman named Mrs. Templeton. Frankie suspects that Roger Baskington-French impersonated Savage to sign a fraudulent will.

The investigation leads them to a confrontation at Tudor Cottage, where Bobby and Frankie are ambushed and tied up in an attic. They discover that the man who captured them was not Dr. Nicholson, but an imposter: Roger Baskington-French. Roger had been using forgery and impersonation to manipulate events and protect the criminal interests of himself and Moira. After a struggle, Bobby, Frankie, and their friend Budge Bidden manage to ambush and bind Roger.

The truth is finally revealed through a confession from Roger. He admits to pushing Allen Castles off the cliff to protect Moira's identity and to prevent Castles from contacting a former servant named Evans. He also confesses to murdering Henry Baskington-French to secure his inheritance and attempting to kill Bobby and Frankie. It is revealed that Moira (formerly Mrs. Templeton) and Roger were partners in a massive criminal conspiracy involving drug trafficking and the manipulation of wealthy estates. Moira had used her marriage to Nicholson as a cover and had orchestrated the death of John Savage.

Following the exposure of the conspiracy, Bobby and Frankie, having discovered their mutual affection, decide to leave England to start a new life together on a coffee plantation in Kenya.

title: 奉命谋杀
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\252-奉命谋杀-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Dr. Arthur Calgary, a geophysicist recently returned from an expedition to Antarctica, arrives at "Sun Angle" (also known as Sunset Point or Snake's Head) to meet the Aigle family. His mission is to deliver news that could clear the name of Jack Aigle, the son of Leo Aigle and the late Rachel Aigle. Jack had been convicted of murdering his mother with a poker and died in prison six months after his sentencing.

Calgary meets Leo, his daughter Hester, and the secretary Gwendolyn Vaughn. He reveals that he was the driver of the car that picked up a hitchhiker on the night of the murder. Because Calgary drove the young man to the center of town at approximately 7:05 p.m., he can provide a definitive alibi for Jack, proving he could not have committed the crime between 7:00 and 7:30 p.m. Calgary had been unable to come forward sooner due to a concussion from a subsequent traffic accident, which caused memory loss.

The news of Jack's innocence causes profound distress rather than relief. The family members—Leo, Hester, Mary Darland, Michael Aigle, and Tina—are all adopted children whom Rachel had brought into her highly controlled, "artificial" family. The revelation that Jack was innocent reopens a cold case, as the murder occurred in a locked house and was likely committed by someone known to the victim. The family's stability is shattered by suspicion and the realization that the true killer remains among them.

As the investigation intensifies, various motives and secrets emerge. Philip Darland, Mary’s husband, begins conducting his own amateur investigation, viewing the situation as an intellectual game. He suspects the killer is a member of the household. Meanwhile, Michael Aigle expresses deep resentment toward his adoptive upbringing, and Mary reflects on her desire to escape her mother's suffocating influence. Tina, another adopted daughter, reveals she was near the house on the night of the murder and overheard two people whispering about a plan to "not mess up" and that "everything will be wonderful."

The tension culminates in a second tragedy. Philip Darland is found stabbed to death in his room. A knife is found in his pocket, and Tina, who discovers the body, is injured and falls into a state of shock. The police take Michael into custody, but Calgary remains unconvinced of Michael's guilt, suspecting a crime of passion.

Calgary returns to Sun Angle to resolve the chaos he believes his arrival initiated. He confronts the group and focuses his attention on Kirsten Lindström, the Swedish nurse and housekeeper. Calgary posits that Jack was not the killer, but the mastermind who manipulated Kirsten. He explains that Jack exploited Kirsten's affection for him to convince her to kill Mrs. Aigle to facilitate a theft. 

Kirsten breaks down and confesses. She admits to killing Mrs. Aigle out of a misguided devotion to Jack, but she also confesses to murdering Philip Darland and stabbing Tina. She acted out of a desperate, panicked desire to protect herself after realizing that Jack had deceived her. As the truth is revealed, the family is left to process the devastation, and Hester expresses a sudden desire to marry Calgary.
